draft.introduction: Firmicus Maternus’s De Errore Profanarum Religionum (c. 346 CE) is a Christian polemic urging the emperors to forcibly suppress pagan worship. Notably, Firmicus himself had been a practicing astrologer before his conversion, lending his attack an insider’s knowledge.

translation.text: The profane religions of the pagans, most sacred emperors, must be utterly destroyed and abolished. Their error has persisted long enough. The madness of a ruinous superstition must be resisted. The elements are not gods; they are the works of God, created to serve the needs of humankind.
translation.original_text: Profanae religiones paganorum, sacratissimi imperatores, penitus destruendae sunt atque abolendae. Diu erravit error iste. Resistendum est furori perniciosae superstitionis.

translator_notes.note: Firmicus represents the militant wing of Christian anti-paganism. His call for imperial suppression anticipates the Theodosian decrees. That he was himself a former pagan practitioner makes De Errore both a conversion narrative and a political program.
